title: In, on, at
description: Learn how to use the prepositions in, on and at for time and place.

topics.title: In
topics.description: Used for months, years, seasons and parts of the day; and for towns/cities, countries/continents, areas/regions, and being inside an object, room or building.

uses.example: Paris is wonderful in April.
I first went to Russia in 2005.
We often go skiing in winter.
My train leaves in the afternoon.
There's a famous castle in Edinburgh.
My brother is in Mexico.
What's life like in the desert?
Your passport is in the drawer.
I've left the tickets in the living room!
Sharon has been in the travel agent's for an hour!

notes.content: We also use in in the following phrases.
With verbs of motion such as come, go, move, run and walk, we usually use to instead of in, on or at.
examples: Was it hot when you went to Japan?

topics.title: On
topics.description: Used for days and dates; and for islands, pages, the top of an object, and surfaces.

uses.example: I got a new car on Saturday.
My birthday is on 19th March.
Last year, we stayed on Mykonos.
There are some useful Italian phrases on page 97.
Did you put your car keys on the kitchen table?
There's a timetable on the wall.

notes.content: We also use on in the following phrases.
We say in the morning/afternoon/evening, but on Monday morning/Wednesday evening, etc.
examples: We're flying to Washington in the morning / on Tuesday morning.

notes.content: We don't use a preposition with tomorrow, yesterday, tomorrow morning, yesterday evening, etc.
examples: We're flying to Washington tomorrow afternoon.

topics.title: At
topics.description: Used for clock times and holiday periods; and for exact places, addresses, buildings (talking about the activities that happen there), and activities.

uses.example: There's a bus at ten past three.
What are you doing at Christmas?
What's it like at the North Pole?
My cousin lives at 132 London Road.

uses.usage: Buildings, when talking about the activities that happen there
uses.example: I think John is at the cinema, watching Titanic.
Rania isn't here. She's at a party.

notes.content: We also use at in the following phrases.
Compare how we use in and at for places: we use in for larger areas that are all around us when we are there, and at for smaller places and points on a journey.
examples: We're spending our next holiday in the countryside.
Let's meet at the train station.
